text.title: Vector DB Monitoring — Core Rules
text.summary: Five rules: four-pillar metric coverage, paged-vs-ticket routing, capacity baseline, SLO panel on dashboard, runbook link required on every alert.

rule.statement: Monitoring MUST cover four pillars: latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate (%), memory (used/cap), disk (used/cap). Missing any pillar leaves a blind spot.
rule.rationale: Each pillar catches a different failure class. Memory pressure manifests as latency spike before OOM; disk pressure before write rejection.

rule.statement: Alert routing MUST distinguish page (immediate action required) from ticket (review at next sync). Both classes must be defined in monitoring.yaml.
rule.rationale: Conflating severities causes either alert fatigue (everything pages) OR missed incidents (everything tickets). Explicit routing is the bright line.

rule.statement: Memory + disk growth MUST be tracked vs a baseline window. Forecast OOM date at current growth rate; alert when forecast <30 days.
rule.rationale: Reactive scaling is too late on vector DBs (re-index takes hours). Trend-based capacity alerts give multi-week lead time.

rule.statement: Grafana dashboard MUST display the SLO targets on the same panel as the measured value (p95_latency < SLO_p95 etc.). Naked metrics without SLO context are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Operators need to know "is this normal?" at a glance. SLO bars make breach visible without mental math.

rule.statement: Every alert rule MUST include a `runbook_url` annotation pointing to an actionable doc. Alerts without runbooks are forbidden.
rule.rationale: On-call response time drops 60% when runbooks are 1 click away. Bare alerts force on-call to debug from zero context.
